e demons and the Gods.
[The Moon is said to be inimical to the lotuses which are
delighted at the rising of the Sun. Here the idea is that the banner
would enable the celestials to trounce the demons. These verses
represent our author's good poetry. Mark the fine alliteration and
description in the second line, paradox in the third a.r\(\yathftsahkhya
in the last line.]
Indra was overjoyed on receiving that banner, which was
born of Visnu's effulgence, which shone brilliantly like the blazing
Sun in autumn, and which was mounted on a glittering eight-
wheeled chariot, bedecked with jewels.
tmgrammatical in form The author might have
employed the word instead. The commentator too commits a
similar mistake by using instead of The second line
that contains a beautiful simile reminds us of Valmlki's verse,
V. 2-68.
This and the following verse are in metre.]
The Lord of Gods i.e. Indra, put the army of the enemies to
death in battle with the help of the banner, which was erected
